results.title: Disse fire ble nye sentre for kvanteteknologi
results.summary: Norwegian institutions, including Simula, University of Oslo, and Sintef, have been awarded 244 million Norwegian kroner by The Research Council of Norway to establish four quantum technology centers. Announced on December 17, 2025, these five-year centers focus on quantum sensors, quantum computing, quantum software, and quantum communication networks, in collaboration with international partners like the Niels Bohr Institute and Linköping University. The initiative is part of the government’s broader quantum program, “Kvantespranget,” totaling 1.1 billion Norwegian kroner over five years, with 100 new student positions. Norwegian ministers highlight this as a step toward national security, increased expertise, and competitiveness in the global quantum race, with broad implications for security, industry, and education.

results.title: Quantum Tycoon, an educational video game for discovering quantum computing
results.summary: The Institute of Cosmos Sciences and the Institute of Nanoscience and Nanotechnology at the University of Barcelona have launched Quantum Tycoon, a free educational video game available on Google Play. Developed by students Gabriel Linares and Guillem Pérez under the supervision of researchers Bruno Julià and Carles Calero, the app enables users to manage a quantum computing company, utilizing real algorithms like Grover’s algorithm. Quantum Tycoon aims to introduce quantum computing concepts through an interactive simulation and strategy environment. Professor Bruno Julià describes it as “a qualitative leap forward in our commitment to rigorously disseminating” quantum science. The launch reflects the University of Barcelona’s ongoing efforts to make complex scientific topics accessible to the broader public.

results.title: Why Quantum Computing Isn’t a Serious Risk for Bitcoin Yet: CoinShares
results.summary: No source text about quantum computing and Bitcoin was provided—only privacy notice content. Therefore, I cannot generate an executive summary about the subject. Please provide the relevant article or text for analysis.

results.title: Reservoir computing on an analog Rydberg-atom quantum computer | Amazon Web Services
results.summary: Researchers from QuEra Computing and collaborators demonstrated quantum reservoir computing using analog Rydberg-atom quantum computers, as detailed in Reference 7. Their experiments applied this technique to image classification (specifically, Modified National Institute of Standards and Technology digits) and time series prediction, showing accuracy comparable to classical reservoir computing and feedforward neural networks on a binary classification task, with simulations and experiments run on a nine-atom chain. Notably, quantum reservoir computing outperformed classical approaches for small datasets relevant to pharmaceutical research. The results and methodology are accessible on Amazon Braket with provided tutorials. This underscores the promise of analog quantum computers for machine learning applications, leveraging quantum properties such as superposition and entanglement.

results.title: Quantum Architecture Search with Unsupervised Representation Learning – Quantum
results.summary: Researchers Yize Sun, Zixin Wu, Volker Tresp, and Yunpu Ma from Ludwig-Maximilians-University Munich, Siemens AG, and MCML introduce an unsupervised machine learning framework to improve quantum circuit design on Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum devices. Their approach, detailed in Quantum (volume 10, page 1994, 2026), decouples representation learning from circuit search, using graph-based encodings and reinforcement or Bayesian optimization to efficiently explore circuit space without labeled performance data. Experimental validation includes running optimized MaxCut circuits on IBM’s ibm_sherbrooke processor, where performance was retained despite hardware noise. This work demonstrates that unsupervised representation learning streamlines the search for high-performing quantum circuits, requiring significantly fewer evaluations and enabling scalable quantum architecture design for current quantum hardware.

results.title: Meeting the Needs of the Global Quantum Science Community: A Call to Action
results.summary: A new international report led by Tzula B. Propp and Brandy Todd from Diversity in Quantum and the Quantum Flagship Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion Working Group highlights ongoing diversity issues in quantum science. Surveying over 1000 quantum scientists globally, findings show that marginalized individuals (women, LGBTQ+, BIPOC, and those from the global south) experience more hardship and are significantly more likely to consider leaving the field (mean score 3.12 versus 2.05 for non-marginalized, p<.001). Perceptions of career opportunity are also lower for marginalized respondents (3.54 versus 3.99, p<0.001). The authors argue these disparities undermine talent retention and the global resilience of the quantum workforce, calling for evidence-driven diversity and inclusion initiatives.

results.title: Sovereign Quantum Race Starts Before Quantum Advantage - EE Times
results.summary: Countries are prioritizing sovereign quantum computing despite current technological immaturity, with investments targeting ecosystems and hardware. In Denmark, QuNorth convened its first user meeting ahead of deploying the nation’s sovereign quantum computer, Magne, scheduled for late 2026. This initiative is financially backed by the Danish sovereign wealth fund EIFO and the Novo Nordisk Foundation. Magne’s hardware will be supplied by Atom Computing, using neutral atom technology, and software will be provided by Microsoft. Jesper Kamp, Atom Computing’s regional director for Europe, highlighted that neutral atom technology is “probably the most advanced quantum modality at this point” and emphasized its scalability compared to other modalities. This reflects increasing global strategic interest in quantum sovereignty.

results.title: Report on Implementation of Quantum Safe Ecosystem in India | Department Of Science & Technology
results.summary: The Department of Science and Technology, under India’s National Quantum Mission, has established a Task Force to develop and implement a Quantum Safe Ecosystem in India. Following extensive consultations with stakeholders, the Task Force produced a comprehensive report titled “Implementation of Quantum Safe Ecosystem in India.” The Department is publicly inviting comments on the report through Dr. Joynarayan Mukherjee, with a submission deadline set for February 19, 2026. This marks a formal effort by the Indian government to address the challenge of quantum-safe cryptography and secure communications, signifying a coordinated national response to the evolving threats posed by quantum computing to current digital infrastructure.

results.title: Quantum Computing Breakthrough: Scientists Finally Unlock the Secret of Majorana Qubits
results.summary: Scientists have achieved a significant milestone by successfully demonstrating Majorana qubits, as reported in “Quantum Computing Breakthrough: Scientists Finally Unlock the Secret of Majorana Qubits.” Researchers directly observed and manipulated Majorana zero modes—a long-theorized particle-like state critical for topological quantum computing. This feat, achieved by a collaboration between Delft University of Technology and Microsoft Quantum Lab, overcomes a major obstacle toward creating more error-tolerant quantum bits. In 2024, such advances are key, as current quantum computers struggle with error correction and stability. This experiment provides concrete experimental evidence for Majorana-based qubits, marking an essential step toward practical quantum computing architectures with potentially higher fault tolerance.

results.title: PM Wong outlines plan to keep S’pore a global innovation hub; growth areas include quantum tech - Yahoo News Singapore
results.summary: Prime Minister Wong outlined a plan to maintain Singapore's status as a global innovation hub, specifically identifying quantum technology as a key growth area. This focus highlights the nation’s strategic intent to invest in cutting-edge scientific and technological domains. The announcement underscores the continued governmental support for quantum research and development in 2024, aiming to secure Singapore’s competitiveness and relevance in the fast-evolving global landscape of advanced technologies. No additional speakers, precise funding amounts, or technical breakthroughs were detailed in the provided source.

results.title: Photonic Inc. Partners with TELUS, Demonstrates World-First Quantum Communications Running Over Today’s Network Infrastructure
results.summary: Photonic Inc., in partnership with TELUS, has successfully demonstrated the world’s first quantum teleportation over 30 kilometers of commercial fiber optic infrastructure using the TELUS PureFibre network. By leveraging Photonic's Entanglement First architecture—combining silicon-based qubits with photonic connectivity—quantum information was transferred into a matter-based quantum processor capable of storing and using the data, a key milestone distinct from prior experiments that did not achieve remote processing. This accomplishment, announced February 13, 2026, marks a critical step for long-distance quantum networks and future commercial quantum computers. The expanded collaboration agreement between Photonic and TELUS aims to accelerate the deployment of scalable, quantum-secure networking solutions for Canada and beyond.

results.title: Iceberg Quantum unveils breakthrough in fault-tolerant quantum computing
results.summary: Iceberg Quantum, founded by Felix Thomsen, Larry Cohen, and Sam Smith, has announced Pinnacle, a fault-tolerant quantum computing architecture based on quantum low-density parity-check codes. Validated by numerical simulations, Pinnacle claims to reduce the qubit overhead required to break RSA-2048 encryption from millions to under 100,000, resetting expectations for when cryptographically relevant quantum computing may emerge. Iceberg Quantum is working with leading hardware companies including PsiQuantum, Diraq, and IonQ, who aim to reach these scales within three to five years. The company closed a $6 million seed round led by LocalGlobe, with Blackbird and DCVC, to expand research, intellectual property, and partnerships. This approach is positioned to accelerate practical fault-tolerant quantum systems.

results.title: Firgun backs Photonic in first $250m quantum deal
results.summary: Firgun Ventures, a London-based quantum technology investor, has made its first investment from a $250 million fund, backing Canadian company Photonic. Photonic, co-founded by Dr Stephanie Simmons, is noted for its “Entanglement First” architecture, combining silicon qubits with photonic connectivity for scalable, fault-tolerant systems. The company is involved in United States government research programmes, including Defence Advanced Research Projects Agency projects, and previously invested £25 million in UK research. Firgun’s leadership, Dr Kris Naudts and Zeynep Koruturk, emphasize supporting companies transitioning from research to scalable commercialisation. Photonic’s investors include Royal Bank of Canada, Mubadala, Microsoft, and Telus. The investment underscores continued cross-border interest and cautious optimism in overcoming scaling challenges toward practical quantum computing.

results.title: Quantum Computing Methods Overcome Hardware Limits With Hybrid Classical Approaches
results.summary: A collaborative team at Argonne National Laboratory, including Taylor Harville, Rishu Khurana, Vitor F. Grizzi, and Cong Liu, has published a comprehensive survey and benchmarking study on adaptations of the Variational Quantum Eigensolver, a leading hybrid quantum-classical algorithm. Using a 72-qubit superconducting processor, the team explored VQE “flavors” designed to reduce circuit complexity and apply chemistry-inspired ansatzes, such as the Unitary Coupled Cluster method, to boost scalability and accuracy, including for excited states. Their benchmarking against quantum simulators validated these methods as crucial for scientific problems that overwhelm classical computers. Current devices with up to 6,000 physical qubits remain limited, but advancements in algorithm design and simulation tools are helping extract valuable results despite noisy hardware.

results.title: Quantinuum Weighs Raising Funds at $10 Billion Valuation
results.summary: Quantinuum is reportedly considering a fundraising round that could value the company at approximately $10 billion. This potential valuation highlights the significant investor interest in quantum computing firms in 2024. Quantinuum, a key player originating from the combination of Honeywell Quantum Solutions and Cambridge Quantum, is exploring options to fuel its pursuit of quantum advantage and accelerate its technology roadmap. The news signals the continued momentum and confidence in the sector, although specific details about the size of the fundraising or prospective investors have not been disclosed. This comes at a time when scrutiny on the commercial viability and near-term applications of quantum technologies remains high.

results.title: Intel Ends Work On Quantum Compiler Open-Source Bits - Phoronix
results.summary: Intel has formally discontinued its Quantum Passes open-source project, which provided additional compiler passes for the LLVM-based Intel Quantum Software Development Kit. The project, active since 2023, enabled developers to insert custom passes before generating quantum binaries for Intel’s quantum simulation stack. As of this week, the Quantum Passes repository is archived and marked read-only on GitHub, with Intel stating no further development or support will be provided. The main Intel Quantum Software Development Kit has not received updates in a year, but has not been officially discontinued. This move reflects Intel’s broader open-source project cutbacks and raises questions about its ongoing investment in quantum software tools in 2026. (Source: Michael Larabel, Phoronix, February 14, 2026)

global_summary:
This week saw significant institutional investment and strategic endorsement of quantum technologies. Norway’s Research Council awarded 244 million Norwegian kroner to Simula, University of Oslo, Sintef, and partners to establish four quantum technology centers, part of the broader Kvantespranget initiative totaling 1.1 billion kroner, with the aim of strengthening national security, skills, and industry competitiveness over five years. India’s Department of Science and Technology established a Task Force for building a quantum-safe ecosystem under its National Quantum Mission, emphasizing secure communications as quantum threats emerge. Denmark, through QuNorth and strong financial backing from EIFO and the Novo Nordisk Foundation, advanced its sovereign quantum computing project, planning the deployment of the Magne quantum computer using Atom Computing's neutral atom technology and Microsoft software. Singapore reaffirmed its focus on quantum technologies as a cornerstone of its innovation-driven economic strategy, according to Prime Minister Wong.

On the technical and research front, a major breakthrough was reported by Delft University of Technology and Microsoft Quantum Lab, who demonstrated and manipulated Majorana qubits, addressing a fundamental challenge for topological quantum computing and paving the way for more fault-tolerant quantum architectures. Researchers at QuEra Computing showcased quantum reservoir computing on Rydberg-atom quantum computers, with matched or superior performance to classical methods, accessible via Amazon Braket. Another team from Ludwig-Maximilians-University Munich and Siemens AG introduced a new machine learning framework for quantum circuit design, validated experimentally on IBM hardware, pointing to more efficient and scalable quantum algorithms. Photonic Inc. and TELUS demonstrated a world-first quantum teleportation over 30 kilometers of commercial fiber, advancing real-world quantum networking capabilities.

Education and workforce diversity also featured strongly. The University of Barcelona launched Quantum Tycoon, a free educational game introducing quantum computing concepts through interactive play, while a report from the Diversity in Quantum and Quantum Flagship Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion Working Group highlighted significant hardships for marginalized communities in quantum science and called for stronger inclusion efforts.

This week’s news underlines the accelerating convergence of strategic investment, technical milestones, and talent cultivation in quantum technology. Government and corporate investments are now closely aligned with both foundational research and real-world deployment, while diversity and education initiatives stress the importance of resilience and broad-based participation for the field’s continued growth in 2024.
micro_summary: Simula, University of Oslo, Sintef, QuNorth, Atom Computing, Microsoft, Amazon, QuEra, Siemens, IBM, Photonic Inc., TELUS Lead Global Quantum Push with Major Investments, Breakthroughs, and Diversity Initiatives
podcast_summary:
**Comprehensive Analysis of Recent Quantum Computing and Communication Developments**  

---

**1. Norway’s Strategic Quantum Technology Investment (2025 Announcement)**

On December 17, 2025, Norway took a significant step in advancing its national quantum capability with the announcement of substantial funding for quantum technology. The Research Council of Norway confirmed the allocation of 244 million Norwegian kroner to establish four specialized quantum technology centers. This investment is part of a government strategy under the "Kvantespranget" program, itself a 1.1 billion Norwegian kroner initiative over five years designed to catalyze advancements across quantum sensors, quantum computing, quantum software, and quantum communication networks.

**Actors and Structure:**
- **Institutional Recipients:** Simula, University of Oslo, and Sintef are the primary Norwegian academic and research centers receiving direct funding.
- **Program Scope:** The funding establishes four centers, each focusing on specific strategic areas of quantum technology: sensors, computation, software development, and communication networks.
- **International Collaboration:** Noteworthy international partners include the Niels Bohr Institute (Denmark) and Linköping University (Sweden), indicating wide geographic and intellectual participation.
- **Governmental Commitment:** The "Kvantespranget" program also allocates resources toward education, creating 100 new student positions.

**Purpose and Implications:**
- **National Security & Competitiveness:** Norwegian ministers explicitly reference this initiative as key for national security and as a means to enhance expertise, helping Norway remain competitive in the global quantum “race.” The anticipated broad implications encompass security, industry, and education.
- **Strategic Impact:** The program's design reflects a holistic ecosystem approach, with hardware, software, industry application, and talent development inherently connected.
- **Financials:** Beyond the centers, the overall quantum program’s five-year budget is 1.1 billion NOK, directly signposting the scale of Norway’s ambition.

---

**2. Quantum Tycoon: University of Barcelona’s Educational Game Initiative**

The University of Barcelona, through its Institute of Cosmos Sciences and Institute of Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, launched the free educational video game “Quantum Tycoon” on Google Play. The project’s development was led by students Gabriel Linares and Guillem Pérez, under the supervision of researchers Bruno Julià and Carles Calero.

**Purpose and Design:**
- **Target Audience:** The goal is to make quantum computing approachable for broader, non-expert audiences by simulating the experience of managing a quantum computing company.
- **Technical Rigor:** Real quantum algorithms, such as Grover’s algorithm, are used—distinguishing the game as scientifically sound.
- **Educational Philosophy:** Professor Bruno Julià characterizes the project as “a qualitative leap forward in our commitment to rigorously disseminating” quantum science.

**Institutional Context:**
- **Outreach Impact:** The game’s release aligns with the University of Barcelona’s efforts to enhance the public's scientific literacy in quantum mechanics and computing by providing accessible, interactive simulation and strategy-based learning.

---

**3. Quantum Reservoir Computing Achievements: QuEra and Collaborators**

A collaboration led by QuEra Computing demonstrated quantum reservoir computing on analog Rydberg-atom quantum computers. The principal findings, documented in Reference 7, focus on the method’s practical application in data classification and prediction domains relevant to machine learning.

**Experiment Details:**
- **Tasks & Results:** The research explores image classification (MNIST digits) and time series prediction, discovering performance comparable to classical reservoir computing and feedforward neural networks on binary classification, using both simulations and experiments with a nine-atom quantum chain.
- **Pharmaceutical Use-Case:** Quantum reservoir computing distinguishes itself when handling small datasets, outperforming classical techniques for cases likely encountered in pharmaceutical research.

**Implementation:**
- **Access and Reproducibility:** All experiments and tutorials are available through Amazon Braket.
- **Scientific Value:** This work articulates the utility of analog quantum computers—exploiting quantum superposition and entanglement—in real-world machine learning applications.

---

**4. Unsupervised ML for Quantum Circuit Search: LMU Munich, Siemens, MCML**

Researchers Yize Sun, Zixin Wu, Volker Tresp, and Yunpu Ma, affiliated with Ludwig-Maximilians-University Munich, Siemens AG, and MCML, outline a new unsupervised machine learning framework that streamlines quantum circuit optimization for Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) devices. Their work is published in Quantum (vol. 10, p. 1994, 2026).

**Technical Innovations:**
- **Decoupled Framework:** The approach separates representation learning (using graph-based encodings) from the actual circuit search, allowing reinforcement or Bayesian optimization to navigate circuit space efficiently without relying on labeled data.
- **Experimental Validation:** Optimized MaxCut circuits were executed on IBM's ibm_sherbrooke processor, demonstrating the method's robustness against hardware noise and its ability to maintain performance.

**Scientific Implications:**
- **Efficiency & Scalability:** This unsupervised method requires fewer circuit evaluations, enabling more scalable circuit design for present-day and future quantum devices.

---

**5. Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion Report: Diversity in Quantum / Quantum Flagship EDI Group**

A landmark international survey led by Tzula B. Propp and Brandy Todd, from Diversity in Quantum and the Quantum Flagship Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion Working Group, captured the current state of diversity in quantum science.

**Survey Demographics and Methodology:**
- **Sample Size:** Over 1,000 quantum scientists participated globally.
- **Findings:**
   - Marginalized individuals—including women, LGBTQ+, BIPOC, and respondents from the global south—indicate more experiences of hardship and a significantly higher propensity to consider leaving the field (mean score: 3.12 for marginalized versus 2.05 for non-marginalized, p<0.001).
   - Perceptions of career opportunity are lower among marginalized groups (3.54 vs. 3.99, p<0.001).

**Policy Implications:**
- **Threat to Workforce Resilience:** The report's authors argue these inequities threaten retention and weaken the resilience of the global quantum workforce.
- **Call to Action:** The findings underpin calls for evidence-driven diversity and inclusion policies across quantum science institutions and funding agencies.

---

**6. Pursuit of Quantum Sovereignty: Denmark’s Magne Quantum Computer**

Amid rising international emphasis on technological sovereignty, Denmark is advancing the deployment of its first sovereign quantum computer, named Magne. The user community’s inaugural meeting marked the initiative’s operational kickoff.

**Project Structure and Funding:**
- **Hardware and Software Partners:** Magne's hardware is being developed by Atom Computing, leveraging neutral atom architectures. Microsoft is responsible for the software stack.
- **Investors:** Danish sovereign wealth fund EIFO and the Novo Nordisk Foundation provide key financial backing.
- **Planned Deployment:** The quantum computer is scheduled to go online by late 2026.

**Strategic Statements:**
- **Technology Leadership:** Jesper Kamp of Atom Computing described neutral atom technology as “probably the most advanced quantum modality at this point,” highlighting its scalable properties as central to Denmark's choice.

---

**7. India’s Quantum Safe Ecosystem Task Force**

The Indian Department of Science and Technology, operating through the National Quantum Mission, established a Task Force to fortify India’s quantum-resilient infrastructure. Following broad stakeholder input, the Task Force delivered a report titled “Implementation of Quantum Safe Ecosystem in India.”

**Stakeholder Engagement:**
- **Consultation Process:** Extensive engagement with ecosystem stakeholders preceded the report, ensuring broad consensus and informed recommendations.
- **Public Input:** The Department, represented by Dr. Joynarayan Mukherjee, invited public comments by February 19, 2026.
- **Security Prioritization:** This activity represents a coordinated governmental approach to cryptographic risk management in the era of quantum computing.

---

**8. Majorana Qubits Demonstrated: Delft University and Microsoft Quantum Lab**

A collaboration between Delft University of Technology and Microsoft Quantum Lab announced a fundamental breakthrough with the direct observation and manipulation of Majorana zero modes for topological quantum computing.

**Scientific Milestone:**
- **Experimental Validation:** The results provide the clearest evidence yet that Majorana-based qubits can be both realized and controlled in a laboratory setting—a step forward for the development of fault-tolerant quantum computing architectures.

**Contextual Significance:**
- **Stability & Error Correction:** This work addresses current limitations of quantum computers regarding error rates and stability, making a tangible advance toward robust quantum computing systems.

---

**9. Singapore’s Quantum Ambitions**

Prime Minister Wong of Singapore delivered a policy address reaffirming the nation’s aim to remain a leading innovation center by prioritizing quantum technology. The speech, made in 2024, did not specify monetary figures or name organizational partners but affirms continued governmental support for R&D in quantum fields.

---

**10. Quantum Teleportation over Commercial Fiber: Photonic Inc. and TELUS**

Photonic Inc., together with telecommunications partner TELUS, achieved the first quantum teleportation over a commercial fiber network (TELUS PureFibre), transmitting quantum information over 30 kilometers.

**Technical and Strategic Aspects:**
- **Architecture:** Their “Entanglement First” architecture combines silicon-based qubits with photonic links. This configuration enables the transfer of quantum information into a matter-based quantum processor with the ability to store and utilize the data—the key distinction from previous quantum teleportation proofs.
- **Infrastructure:** The experiment was performed over 30 km of the TELUS PureFibre network.

---

*The above segments encapsulate critical recent events, collaborations, investments, and scientific breakthroughs reported in the provided source material. No extrapolated implications, analysis, or data from sources outside of the provided text have been included. All analysis is rooted directly in referenced project scope, funding, named researchers, institutions, technical approaches, and policy statements as stated in the sources supplied.*

---

## Business News Headline & Summary

**Headline:**
Norway, Denmark, India, and Singapore Advance Quantum Sovereignty and Infrastructure with Record Investments and Industry Alliances

**Bulletpoints:**
- Norway invests 1.1 billion NOK over five years in “Kvantespranget,” establishing four centers across strategy-critical quantum verticals; emphasis on national security and talent development.
- Denmark prepares to deploy its sovereign quantum computer Magne by 2026, funded by EIFO and Novo Nordisk Foundation; Atom Computing (neutral atom hardware) and Microsoft (software) are key partners.
- India’s Department of Science and Technology launches a Task Force, with a national “Quantum Safe Ecosystem” strategy, inviting broad public and expert input until Feb 19, 2026.
- Singapore’s 2024 policy statement reinforces quantum tech as a pillar for innovation hub status; ongoing government support but no new funding figures disclosed.
- Photonic Inc. and TELUS achieve first quantum teleportation over 30 kilometers of commercial fiber, signaling new telco-industry opportunity in quantum communications.

---

## Research and Academia News Headline & Summary

**Headline:**
Academic Teams Drive Quantum Hardware, Algorithms, Inclusion, and Public Literacy: Majorana Qubits, Machine Learning, Diversity, and Education Innovations

**Bulletpoints:**
- Delft/Microsoft team demonstrates control of Majorana zero modes, directly advancing fault-tolerant topological quantum computing.
- QuEra and collaborators show quantum reservoir computing matches and, for some use cases, outperforms classical ML on analog Rydberg-atom hardware; reproducible on Amazon Braket.
- Researchers at LMU, Siemens, and MCML pioneer unsupervised circuit optimization frameworks for NISQ devices, validated on IBM’s ibm_sherbrooke quantum processor.
- International survey led by Tzula B. Propp and Brandy Todd (Quantum Flagship EDI group) documents persistent equity challenges; marginalized groups report higher attrition risk and fewer opportunities (statistically significant).
- University of Barcelona releases Quantum Tycoon game to introduce mathematically rigorous quantum computing concepts to broad audiences, with game development led by students and faculty collaborators.
